text.title: Feature-to-Benefit Translation, Objection Handling, and CTAs
text.summary: Feature→So what→Benefit formula, objection response patterns, CTA best practices, and before/after examples.

rule.statement: Apply the "Feature → So what? → Benefit" formula to every product feature before writing any copy.
rule.rationale: Features describe the product; benefits describe what the customer gets. Readers process "what's in it for me" — features alone do not answer that question.

example.code: 256-bit encryption → So what? → Your data is bank-level secure AI-powered → So what? → Get answers in seconds, not hours Cloud-based → So what? → Access from anywhere, no setup required 24/7 support → So what? → Help whenever you need it, not just business hours

example.note: Feature-only copy that fails to connect
example.code: "Our software uses advanced AI algorithms to analyze your marketing data and provide insights through a customizable dashboard with real-time updates."

example.note: Benefit-first rewrite of the same product
example.code: "Stop guessing which marketing works. Get clear answers in 60 seconds. No spreadsheets. No analyst needed."

rule.statement: Address the top 3-5 objections explicitly in copy; never assume the reader will overcome objections on their own.
rule.rationale: Unaddressed objections are silent conversion killers. Readers leave rather than email questions. Anticipating objections in copy removes the blocker at the point of decision.

list.item: "Too expensive" → Show ROI, compare cost to the alternative (doing it manually, the competitor)
"Don't have time" → Quantify time saved ("15 minutes, not 3 hours")
"Not sure it'll work" → Testimonials, case studies with specific numbers
"Not for me" → Address their specific role and situation directly
"Can I trust you?" → Social proof, money-back guarantee, number of customers

rule.statement: Every piece of copy must have exactly one CTA; multiple CTAs reduce conversion by creating decision paralysis.
rule.rationale: Each additional call to action reduces the probability of any action being taken. One CTA maintains focus on the single desired outcome.

rule.statement: Write the CTA button text before writing the body copy; it forces clarity about what action you are driving.
rule.rationale: Knowing the end action shapes the entire copy arc. Writing body copy first often leads to CTAs that do not match the content's actual offer.

list.item: Include a benefit hint: "Get My Free Guide" not "Click Here"
Reduce perceived commitment: "See How It Works" before "Buy Now"

example.code: Submit → Get My Free Guide Click Here → Start My 14-Day Trial Sign Up → Create Your Account (Free) Learn More → See How It Works Buy Now → Get Instant Access

section.title: Editing Checklist

list.item: Read aloud — awkward phrasing surfaces immediately
Cut every word that does not add information (no filler)
Simplify sentences — break any sentence over 20 words into two
Check "you" vs "we" ratio — target 2:1 in favor of "you"
Verify one idea per paragraph — split if two ideas appear

section.reference: Python script to score copy with textstat (Flesch, Fog, sentence length)
Landing page structure from headline through risk reversal
Email copy structure with subject, hook, benefits, CTA, P.S.
